stem: A 6-year-old girl with biopsy-naive nephrotic syndrome diagnosed 5 weeks ago has been on oral prednisolone 60 mg/m2 daily since presentation. Her initial presentation was typical: oedema, UPCR 520 mg/mmol, albumin 16 g/L, normal C3 and C4, no haematuria, normal blood pressure and normal renal function. She has remained compliant per home dosing diary and parental observation. At 4 weeks she remained heavily proteinuric (UPCR 460 mg/mmol) and albumin 19 g/L; methylprednisolone pulses 600 mg/m2 IV daily for 3 days were given according to local protocol with no remission. She is now Cushingoid, has BP 124/78 mmHg (over the 95th centile), urea 7.2 mmol/L, creatinine 64 micromol/L (baseline 30), albumin 18 g/L, UPCR 480 mg/mmol. Repeat C3 and C4 are normal. Hepatitis B and C serology, HIV, ANA and dsDNA are all negative. She has had no recent infections. Family history is negative for renal disease and there is no consanguinity. She is fully immunised including varicella and pneumococcal vaccines. Paediatric nephrology is reviewing her in clinic this afternoon, and the parents are asking about prognosis and alternatives. Renal ultrasound shows normal-sized kidneys with normal corticomedullary differentiation and no thrombosis on Doppler imaging.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue current prednisolone dose for a further 4 weeks and reassess
options.rationale: Failure to remit after 4-6 weeks of daily prednisolone (with or without IV methylprednisolone pulses) defines steroid resistance; prolonging steroid monotherapy exposes the child to toxicity without benefit (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).

options.text: Commence empirical calcineurin inhibitor (tacrolimus) without further investigation
options.rationale: Calcineurin inhibitors are second-line therapy but should be initiated after biopsy and genetic results, since monogenic causes respond poorly and biopsy may reveal alternative pathology (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).

options.text: Arrange percutaneous renal biopsy with genetic testing for podocyte gene panel
options.rationale: Steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome requires biopsy to characterise histology (commonly FSGS, less often MCD or membranous) and genetic testing for monogenic podocytopathies (NPHS1, NPHS2, WT1) which predicts response to second-line immunosuppression (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome; KHA-CARI - Nephrotic syndrome in children).

options.text: Switch to rituximab induction and taper prednisolone over 12 weeks
options.rationale: Rituximab is used in frequently-relapsing or steroid-dependent disease and selected steroid-resistant cases, but only after biopsy and confirmation of histology and genetic status (KDIGO 2021 - Nephrotic syndrome).

options.text: Add ACE inhibitor and cease steroids given Cushingoid features
options.rationale: ACEi is adjunctive antiproteinuric therapy in SRNS but immunosuppression cannot be ceased abruptly without a confirmed histological diagnosis and a second-line plan; abrupt cessation also risks adrenal insufficiency (KDIGO 2021 - Glomerular diseases).

stem: A 3-year-old girl is admitted from a regional ED after 6 days of bloody diarrhoea attributed to a daycare gastro outbreak. Stool PCR has confirmed Shiga toxin-producing E. coli O157. On day 7 of illness she becomes pale and oliguric with worsening lethargy and one episode of seizure-like jerks lasting 30 seconds. She had been managed with oral rehydration; she received no antibiotics or antimotility agents. On examination she is pale, has periorbital puffiness, scattered petechiae, T 36.8 degC, HR 132, BP 138/86 mmHg (above the 99th centile for age and height), cap refill 3 s. Bloods: Hb 72 g/L, platelets 38 x10^9/L, blood film shows schistocytes and burr cells, LDH 1620 U/L, haptoglobin undetectable, direct Coombs negative, reticulocytes raised. Urea 24 mmol/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 35), sodium 132, potassium 5.4, bicarbonate 17, INR 1.1, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L. Coagulation profile is normal. Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 2+, granular casts present. She is alert between observation rounds. The paediatric registrar is weighing initial supportive measures with the on-call nephrologist. Renal ultrasound shows normal-sized echogenic kidneys with no obstruction; chest X-ray demonstrates a small bilateral pleural effusion without consolidation.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management decision?

options.text: Transfuse one adult-dose platelet pool prophylactically to reduce bleeding risk from petechiae
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion in STEC-HUS may exacerbate microvascular thrombosis and is avoided unless there is active major bleeding or a planned invasive procedure; thrombocytopenia from platelet consumption resolves with renal and haematological recovery (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome; Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).

options.text: Initiate urgent plasma exchange with fresh frozen plasma replacement therapy daily
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is the treatment for TTP and atypical HUS, not STEC-HUS, where it has not shown benefit and is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty pending ADAMTS13 results (Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous ceftriaxone to cover ongoing Shiga toxin-producing E. coli
options.rationale: Antibiotic treatment of STEC infection is associated with increased risk of HUS progression because of toxin release and is not recommended once HUS is established or in the acute STEC colitis phase (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

options.text: Cautious isotonic fluids, transfuse red cells for symptomatic anaemia, avoid platelets
options.rationale: Management of STEC-HUS is supportive: cautious isotonic fluids to maintain euvolaemia (avoid overload in oliguric phase), red cell transfusion for symptomatic or severe anaemia (typically Hb <60-70 g/L), antihypertensive treatment, and avoidance of platelet transfusion unless active major bleeding because of microthrombus risk (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

options.text: Give an antimotility agent such as loperamide to reduce diarrhoeal losses
options.rationale: Antimotility agents are contraindicated in STEC infection because they prolong toxin contact with the gut and increase HUS risk and severity (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

stem: A 4-year-old boy with day 9 of confirmed Shiga toxin-producing E. coli HUS is on the paediatric ward with worsening biochemistry over 24 hours despite cautious fluid restriction and one red cell transfusion. He has been anuric for 18 hours. He is alert but listless and complaining of nausea. On examination he has scattered petechiae, periorbital and pretibial oedema, mild bibasal crackles, T 36.8 degC, HR 124, RR 38, BP 142/92 mmHg, SpO2 96 percent on room air, weight 18.4 kg (up from admission weight of 17.8 kg). Bloods now: Hb 78 g/L, platelets 42 x10^9/L, urea 38 mmol/L, creatinine 320 micromol/L, sodium 128, potassium 6.8 mmol/L, bicarbonate 12, pH 7.20, calcium 1.85 mmol/L corrected, phosphate 2.6, lactate 1.8. ECG shows peaked T waves in V2-V5 with QRS still 90 ms. He has been given IV calcium gluconate, insulin-dextrose and salbutamol with only partial response (repeat K 6.4). Chest X-ray shows interstitial oedema. The on-call nephrologist is being phoned. He has no central venous access yet. Renal ultrasound shows normal-sized echogenic kidneys with no obstruction and a small bladder. He has had no seizure activity and remains arousable but is increasingly tachypnoeic with subjective dyspnoea on minimal exertion.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat IV calcium gluconate and continue medical therapy for hyperkalaemia
options.rationale: Medical management of hyperkalaemia provides only temporary shift and does not address fluid overload, acidosis or persistent anuria; failure to respond is itself a dialysis indication (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury; KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

options.text: Commence furosemide infusion to drive a urine output of 1 mL/kg/hr
options.rationale: Diuretics are unlikely to be effective in established anuric AKI and are not a substitute for renal replacement when AEIOU dialysis indications are met (KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

options.text: Give 0.9 percent sodium chloride bolus 10 mL/kg to improve renal perfusion
options.rationale: Further fluid in established anuric HUS with rising weight, hypertension and pulmonary oedema worsens overload and does not restore perfusion when the cause is intrinsic glomerular and tubular injury (RCH CPG - Haemolytic uraemic syndrome).

options.text: Initiate acute renal replacement therapy for refractory hyperkalaemia, acidosis and fluid overload
options.rationale: Renal replacement is indicated when any AEIOU criterion is met: refractory acidosis (pH <7.2, bicarbonate <12), refractory electrolyte derangement (K >6.5 not responding to medical therapy), fluid overload causing pulmonary oedema, uraemic symptoms, or oligo/anuria with biochemical deterioration; this child meets multiple criteria (RCH CPG - Acute kidney injury; Australasian Paediatric Nephrology - HUS guidance).

options.text: Add oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate and review potassium in 6 hours
options.rationale: Resonium is slow and risks intestinal necrosis in unwell paediatric patients and does not address acidosis or fluid overload; the situation requires renal replacement (KDIGO 2012 - AKI guideline).

stem: A term, phenotypically male newborn is reviewed in the postnatal ward at 18 hours of age. Pregnancy was uneventful with no maternal androgen exposure. The 20-week scan reported male genitalia. The neonatal examination raises concern. The infant is alert, has stable vitals for age (HR 138, RR 46, SpO2 98 percent RA), capillary refill 2 s, normal cardiac auscultation, soft non-tender abdomen with no masses and no inguinal swellings. Genital examination shows a normal-sized phallus with a glanular meatus, no chordee, but the scrotum is small, mildly rugated and EMPTY bilaterally with NO palpable gonads in either scrotum or along the inguinal canals. There is no hyperpigmentation. The skin is normal and the infant has passed meconium and urine. Family history is unremarkable; there is no consanguinity and no relatives with infertility, salt-wasting illness or ambiguous genitalia. Birth weight is on the 50th centile. The newborn screening blood spot has not been collected yet. The midwife asks whether discharge can proceed in the morning as planned and the parents wish to register the birth as male. He has had no vomiting and feeding is established.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange urgent karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-hydroxyprogesterone, electrolytes and paediatric endocrine review
options.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads in a phenotypic male is a DSD red flag requiring urgent multidisciplinary workup to exclude congenital adrenal hyperplasia (a virilised 46,XX female), partial androgen insensitivity, gonadal dysgenesis or anorchia; investigations include karyotype, pelvic ultrasound, 17-OHP, electrolytes and androgen profile with sex assignment deferred (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development; RCH CPG - Undescended testes).

options.text: Discharge with outpatient paediatric surgical review at 6 weeks for bilateral cryptorchidism
options.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads in a phenotypic male newborn is a DSD until proven otherwise and risks missing salt-wasting CAH presenting in week 2; outpatient deferral is unsafe (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).

options.text: Arrange laparoscopic exploration in the first week to locate intra-abdominal gonads
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is the eventual investigation for confirmed non-palpable testes but only after DSD has been excluded; operating without a karyotype risks intervention on a virilised female (EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

options.text: Commence empirical IM hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone for presumed salt-wasting CAH
options.rationale: Empirical steroid and mineralocorticoid replacement is reserved for biochemically confirmed or strongly suspected adrenal crisis; empirical treatment without workup masks diagnosis and is not first-line in the stable infant (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).

options.text: Reassure parents this is constitutional and recheck at the 6-week well-child examination
options.rationale: Bilateral non-palpable gonads is never a normal variant and reassurance delays critical workup; salt-wasting CAH can be lethal in week 2 if missed (RCH CPG - Disorders of sex development).

stem: A 14-year-old boy attends a metropolitan ED at 02:00 with the third episode in 6 months of acute left testicular pain. Previous episodes lasted 30-90 minutes and resolved spontaneously before he could be assessed. Tonight the pain started 4 hours ago, was severe, associated with vomiting and lower abdominal radiation, and has now partially settled to a moderate ache. He has had no trauma, no urinary symptoms, no urethral discharge and is not sexually active. He is Tanner 4. He has no significant past medical history, takes no regular medications and has no allergies. On arrival he is afebrile with HR 92, BP 118/68, RR 16, SpO2 99 percent. The left hemiscrotum is mildly swollen. The left testis is now in a normal vertical lie, mildly tender diffusely, the cremasteric reflex is present but sluggish on the left and brisk on the right, and there is no erythema. The right testis lies high in the scrotum on a horizontal axis (transverse lie) with a bell-clapper appearance. Urinalysis is negative. A formal scrotal ultrasound with colour Doppler within 30 minutes reports normal symmetric arterial flow bilaterally with a heterogeneous left testis and a small reactive hydrocele. The on-call paediatric surgical fellow is reviewing the case. He is otherwise well and his pain has continued to settle since arrival. Past episodes have always resolved by morning. He has not started antibiotics and no analgesia beyond paracetamol has been given so far.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge with analgesia and outpatient urology review given normal Doppler flow
options.rationale: Recurrent stereotyped episodes of acute scrotal pain with spontaneous resolution and a contralateral bell-clapper appearance are classic for intermittent torsion; normal Doppler does not exclude detorted torsion and discharge risks recurrent torsion with infarction (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain; BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion).

options.text: Admit for observation with serial scrotal examinations and repeat Doppler later
options.rationale: Observation delays definitive treatment and exposes the testis to recurrent torsion-detorsion injury; the contralateral bell-clapper and recurrent history mandate operative exploration (BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion).

options.text: Arrange same-day MRI scrotum to characterise testicular perfusion and architecture
options.rationale: MRI is not the imaging modality of choice in suspected torsion and delays surgical exploration, which remains the safest and most informative investigation when clinical suspicion is high (BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion).

options.text: Proceed to urgent scrotal exploration with bilateral orchidopexy regardless of Doppler
options.rationale: Recurrent stereotyped acute scrotal pain with spontaneous resolution is intermittent torsion; clinical diagnosis trumps imaging because Doppler is performed after detorsion and can be falsely reassuring. Bilateral fixation is required because the bell-clapper anomaly is typically bilateral, and the contralateral testis examination confirms it (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain; BMJ Best Practice - Testicular torsion; EAU Paediatric Urology Guidelines).

options.text: Commence empirical intravenous ceftriaxone and oral doxycycline for presumed epididymo-orchitis
options.rationale: Negative urinalysis, no urethral discharge, no sexual activity and a contralateral bell-clapper testis make epididymo-orchitis unlikely; antibiotics delay definitive operative management of intermittent torsion (RCH CPG - Acute scrotal pain).

stem: A 15-year-old girl presents to a Victorian general practice with her 18-year-old sister. She has confirmed pregnancy at 7 weeks by dating ultrasound today and is requesting termination of pregnancy. The biological father is her 16-year-old boyfriend and the relationship is described by both as consensual and non-coercive. The patient does not want to tell either parent at present and asks specifically about confidentiality. She can describe both medical (mifepristone with misoprostol) and surgical termination options, explains the risks of each in age-appropriate language, and articulates that continuing the pregnancy is not what she wants. She has no medical contraindication to medical termination, is haemodynamically stable, and her social situation is stable with her sister supporting her. Her HEEADSSS screen reveals no self-harm, no substance use, and her mood is appropriately anxious but not depressed. She has no prior pregnancies, takes no medications, and is up to date with the Australian immunisation schedule. There is no Child Protection history. The general practitioner is comfortable with the procedure pathway and the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 framework but is unsure of the consent and confidentiality position for this patient given her age.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer the case to the Victorian Children's Court for authorisation because termination decisions for under 16s require judicial sanction
options.rationale: Termination of pregnancy under the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008 does not require court authorisation for competent minors; Gillick competence is the relevant test, as per RANZCOG.

options.text: Mandatorily report the pregnancy to Victorian Child Protection today because the patient is under 16 and currently pregnant
options.rationale: Pregnancy in an under-16 is not by itself mandatorily reportable in Victoria; the threshold is significant harm and considers age difference, coercion, and exploitation, as per Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting.

options.text: Require written parental consent from at least one parent before any termination referral because Gillick does not apply to termination decisions
options.rationale: Gillick competence applies to all medical decisions including termination of pregnancy; parental consent is encouraged but not required for a competent minor, as per RANZCOG guidance.

options.text: Document Gillick competence today, maintain confidentiality, encourage parental involvement, and refer for the patient's chosen termination pathway
options.rationale: A competent minor may consent to termination of pregnancy in Victoria; the clinician documents Gillick competence, encourages but does not require parental involvement, and refers for the patient's chosen pathway, as per RANZCOG and the Victorian Abortion Law Reform Act 2008.

options.text: Defer the decision by two weeks of additional counselling because the patient is too young to consent in the first trimester
options.rationale: Mandatory cooling off periods are not part of Victorian termination law and delay risks pushing the patient out of the medical termination window, as per RANZCOG.

stem: A paediatric registrar at a metropolitan Victorian public hospital is asked to review an 11-year-old boy admitted after an episode of self-harm at boarding school. During the admission the boy discloses that a residential care worker employed by the boarding school, who is not a teacher, has been entering his bedroom at night for the past three weeks and tickling and wrestling with him in a way that has made him uncomfortable. There has been no disclosed penetrative contact and no injury, but the boy describes the contact as making him anxious and not wanting to return to the school. The boy's parents live overseas and the school is the legal guardian during term. The hospital social worker has been notified and an immediate safety plan has been put in place so the boy does not return to the school until further review. The registrar discusses the case with the consultant paediatrician on call. The hospital has both Child Protection and Police pathways available, and the consultant raises the additional question of whether the conduct falls under the Victorian Reportable Conduct Scheme administered by the Commission for Children and Young People. The school's head of welfare has been informed verbally. The hospital is a mandated entity under Victorian law and the registrar holds a current Working with Children Check.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate combination of statutory pathways for this disclosure?

options.text: Report to Victoria Police and Child Protection only and skip the Reportable Conduct Scheme today because it only covers schoolteachers and not residential care workers
options.rationale: The Reportable Conduct Scheme covers a wide range of organisations including schools and residential care providers, not only classroom teachers, as per the Commission for Children and Young People Victoria.

options.text: Make a Child Protection report alone because the boy is currently admitted to hospital and is safe under medical supervision
options.rationale: Hospital admission does not remove the duty to notify Police of possible criminal conduct or to trigger the Reportable Conduct Scheme for the alleged perpetrator, as per the Commission for Children and Young People Victoria.

options.text: Notify the Department of Families and Victoria Police of possible offending, and have the school notify the Commission under the Reportable Conduct Scheme
options.rationale: Three statutory pathways apply in parallel: Child Protection notification for the child's safety, Police for possible criminal conduct, and the Reportable Conduct Scheme via the head of the entity to the Commission for Children and Young People, as per Better Health Channel and the Commission for Children and Young People Victoria.

options.text: Notify the school principal alone and ask the principal to manage the staff member and any subsequent reports internally
options.rationale: Internal management does not satisfy mandatory reporting or the parallel Reportable Conduct Scheme obligations, both of which sit outside the school's internal HR process, as per Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting.

options.text: Defer all statutory reporting until the boy has completed a forensic interview with the Victorian Forensic Paediatric Medical Service later this week
options.rationale: Mandatory reporting is not deferred for forensic interview; reports are made on reasonable belief and forensic assessment is arranged in parallel, as per Better Health Channel Vic Mandatory Reporting.

stem: A 5-week-old girl is referred to a tertiary paediatric ED by her general practitioner with a 1 cm torn upper labial frenulum that the mother attributes to a recent bottle feed. The baby was born at term by spontaneous vaginal delivery, vitamin K was given, and she has been feeding adequately with appropriate weight gain. She has no other visible injuries and no current bleeding. The parents live together, both are employed, and there has been no previous contact with Child Protection. The infant is fully immunised to age and there is no family history of bleeding disorder, osteogenesis imperfecta, or sudden infant death. On examination there is the torn frenulum, an otherwise normal mouth, no posterior rib tenderness, normal anterior fontanelle, no head circumference change from birth percentile, and a normal neurological exam. Bedside coagulation screen and FBC are normal. The treating team is aware that a torn frenulum in a non-mobile infant is regarded as a sentinel injury and is planning the formal NAI workup according to RCH and Victorian Forensic Paediatric Medical Service guidance. The on-call paediatric consultant and the hospital social worker have been notified, a mandatory report has been submitted to Child Protection, and the infant is now being admitted for full evaluation.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate complete sentinel injury workup for this infant?

options.text: Skeletal survey now and at 11 to 14 days, neuroimaging, dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy, coagulation and bone studies, and forensic paediatric involvement
options.rationale: Standard NAI workup in an infant under 12 months with a sentinel injury includes initial and follow-up skeletal survey, neuroimaging, dilated indirect retinal exam by ophthalmology, bleeding and bone disorder screen, and forensic paediatric assessment, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse and RANZCR.

options.text: Skeletal survey performed once at admission only today, with no follow-up skeletal imaging and no dilated formal eye examination
options.rationale: Repeat skeletal survey at 11-14 days improves detection of healing rib and metaphyseal fractures; single survey alone is incomplete, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse and RANZCR.

options.text: MRI brain only today, with no skeletal imaging and no formal eye examination given the absence of bruising elsewhere on the body
options.rationale: Skeletal survey is required even without external evidence of fractures because clinically silent fractures are common in abused infants, as per RANZCR.

options.text: Bedside eye examination by the paediatric registrar without pupil dilation today, with no skeletal imaging and no brain imaging arranged
options.rationale: Retinal haemorrhage assessment requires dilated indirect ophthalmoscopy by an ophthalmologist; bedside undilated fundoscopy is insufficient, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse.

options.text: Coagulation screen alone and a routine outpatient paediatric follow-up arranged because the injury appears small and isolated today
options.rationale: Sentinel injuries in non-mobile infants are predictive of further serious abuse; outpatient follow-up alone is unsafe, as per RCH CPG: Child Abuse.

stem: A 7-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric developmental clinic for assessment of behavioural and learning difficulties. He was placed in long-term foster care at 3 months of age and his birth mother is documented to have consumed alcohol at hazardous levels throughout the pregnancy. At school he has marked difficulty with executive function and working memory, with school reports describing impulsivity, poor adaptive functioning, and specific maths and language deficits despite intensive support. Cognitive testing performed by an educational psychologist shows a Full Scale IQ of 78 with significant scatter and specific deficits in three of the recognised neurodevelopmental domains (executive function, adaptive behaviour, language). He has met physical growth parameters along the 25th centile. On focused facial examination the paediatrician notes a smooth philtrum, a thin upper vermilion border, and short palpebral fissures (below the 3rd centile for age). Birth history is otherwise unremarkable with a normal newborn examination. There is no history of head injury, no seizure disorder, and no other syndromic features. The foster carer is engaged with the local Aboriginal community-controlled health service for ongoing care.
lead_in: Which Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD diagnostic category best applies?

options.text: Foetal Alcohol Syndrome without confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure because the facial features and growth pattern alone are diagnostic in this case
options.rationale: Without confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure the diagnosis is not made under the Australian Guide; this case has confirmed exposure so the exposure criterion is met, as per the Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD.

options.text: Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder combined presentation alone based on the executive function impairment and impulsivity findings reported today
options.rationale: ADHD may coexist but does not capture the facial dysmorphology and the breadth of severe impairment domains specific to FASD, as per the Australian Guide.

options.text: FASD with less than three sentinel facial features documented today, despite this child being noted to have all three sentinel features on examination
options.rationale: This child meets criteria for three sentinel features and therefore falls into the 3-sentinel-features category, not the less-than-three category, as per the Australian Guide.

options.text: Static encephalopathy of unknown cause because measured IQ is above 70 and prenatal alcohol is not a recognised aetiology in this case
options.rationale: IQ above 70 does not exclude FASD; the diagnosis is made on neurodevelopmental domain impairment, not global IQ alone, as per the Australian Guide.

options.text: FASD with three sentinel facial features, severe impairment in three or more neurodevelopmental domains, and confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure documented
options.rationale: The Australian Guide diagnoses FASD with 3 sentinel facial features when all three are present together with severe impairment in three or more of ten neurodevelopmental domains and confirmed or assumed prenatal alcohol exposure, as per the Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD.

stem: A 15-year-old girl is brought to a tertiary ED by her mother for review after losing 12 kg over five months. She has been progressively restricting her intake, exercising compulsively, and has missed three menstrual periods. She is medically naive and on no medications. On arrival she is alert and oriented, denies suicidal ideation, but is cold to touch and describes near-syncope on standing this morning. Her current weight is 38 kg and her premorbid weight was 50 kg. Body mass index is 14.8 kg/m2 (well below the 3rd centile for age). Her supine heart rate is 38 beats per minute with a postural drop to systolic blood pressure of 78 mmHg standing. Core temperature is 35.4 deg C. Bedside ECG shows sinus bradycardia with a QTc of 470 ms. Bloods reveal potassium 3.0 mmol/L, phosphate 0.7 mmol/L, magnesium 0.65 mmol/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L, and liver enzymes mildly raised with ALT 78 U/L. She has no past psychiatric admission, sees a community psychologist weekly, and her family is supportive and engaged. The on-call paediatrician is applying the Australian and New Zealand Academy for Eating Disorders MARSIPAN-AU criteria to decide on admission.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate disposition?

options.text: Discharge home today with twice-weekly community eating disorder team review and oral potassium and phosphate replacement at home
options.rationale: This patient meets multiple MARSIPAN-AU high risk criteria (bradycardia <40, postural BP drop, hypothermia, hypokalaemia, hypoglycaemia, QTc >450 ms) which mandate inpatient medical admission, as per the ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU Guide.

options.text: Admit to the paediatric ward under medical care with cardiac monitoring, cautious refeeding starting at 20 kcal/kg/day, electrolyte replacement, and ED team involvement
options.rationale: MARSIPAN-AU high risk criteria mandate medical admission with cardiac monitoring, cautious refeeding to prevent refeeding syndrome, electrolyte and glucose management, and dual physical and eating disorder team care, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

options.text: Admit directly to a child and adolescent mental health inpatient unit today with full caloric refeeding at 50 kcal/kg/day starting from day one
options.rationale: Medical stabilisation precedes mental health admission for MARSIPAN-AU high risk patients; aggressive caloric refeeding precipitates refeeding syndrome and is contraindicated, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

options.text: Transfer her today to a peripheral hospital for rapid IV crystalloid resuscitation with 0.9% sodium chloride 20 mL/kg boluses and overnight monitoring
options.rationale: Aggressive crystalloid bolus risks cardiac failure in the malnourished heart; the management is medical admission with measured refeeding rather than resuscitation-style fluids, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.

options.text: Refer her today for outpatient review in an eating disorder day program with weekly bloods, ECG monitoring and a six week follow up plan
options.rationale: Day program review does not address the immediate medical risk; multiple high risk physical criteria require inpatient admission, as per ANZAED MARSIPAN-AU.
